sections.heading: Housing: A Case of Integrated housing development Program (IHDP)
Belsti Asres, Jiayu Pan, Ronita Bardhan, Esayas Alemayehu
sections.text: aSchool of Civil Engineering, Dilla University, Dilla, Po. Box. 419, Ethiopia bDepartment of Architecture, University of Cambridge, CB2 1PX, United Kingdom cFaculty of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Jimma Institute of Technology, Jimma University, Po. Box. 378, Jimma, Ethiopia ARTICLE INFOABSTRACT Keywords:Rising indoor temperatures and associated thermal discomfort, exacerbated by climate change, are growing Thermal comfort’concerns in low-income residential buildings. In Ethiopias Integrated Housing Development Program (IHDP), IHDP housing limited attention has been given to thermal comfort in the design and construction of those affordable housing Indoor environment variables units. This study aims to determine the localized comfort temperature and examine how indoor environmental Adaptive comfort model ’ conditions influence occupantsbehavioral adaptation strategies. Data were collected in Jimma City during dry Adaptive behavior and wet seasons in 2023 through sensor-based environmental monitoring and structured household surveys, analyzed with adaptive comfort models and binary logistic regression. Results indicate an adjusted local comfort ◦ temperature of 25.1 C, with residents demonstrating strong behavioral adaptation to discomfort conditions, particularly through window opening. Both indoor and outdoor temperatures are the most significant predictors of window opening behaviour. When temperatures fall below the localized comfort temperature threshold, the probability of window opening is less than 50%, but it rises sharply to over 90% when temperatures exceed ◦

sections.heading: 30 C. These findings underscore the need to integrate thermal comfort considerations, especially building design
